questions.question: What is the primary goal of cognitive restructuring in CBT?

options.text: To eliminate all negative thoughts and emotions
To test whether automatic thoughts are accurate and generate more balanced alternatives
To replace negative thoughts with positive affirmations
To avoid thinking about situations that cause distress

questions.explanation: Exactly. Cognitive restructuring is about examining evidence to determine if your automatic thought is realistic or distorted — and if distorted, generating a more accurate alternative. The goal is truth, not positivity.

questions.question: In the CBT thought record, what is the purpose of examining 'Evidence AGAINST' the automatic thought?

options.text: To prove that your automatic thought is always wrong
To force yourself to think positively even when you don't believe it
To counteract confirmation bias and consider evidence your brain may have ignored
To suppress negative emotions by denying their validity

questions.explanation: Yes. Your brain naturally seeks evidence that confirms existing beliefs. The 'Evidence AGAINST' step forces you to look for disconfirming evidence — which often exists but was filtered out.

questions.question: When is cognitive restructuring NOT the appropriate intervention?

options.text: When your automatic thought is based on a cognitive distortion like catastrophizing or mind-reading
When your automatic thought is accurate and the situation requires action, not thought change
When your emotion intensity is high
When you're experiencing mild sadness or disappointment

questions.explanation: Correct. If your thought is realistic ('I'm in financial trouble') and the emotion is proportionate, restructuring won't help — you need problem-solving. CR is for distortions, not reality.

questions.question: What is the difference between cognitive restructuring and 'positive thinking'?

options.text: There is no difference — both focus on thinking optimistically
Cognitive restructuring tests thoughts against evidence; positive thinking forces optimism
Positive thinking is more effective for reducing anxiety

questions.explanation: Yes. CR is about accuracy, not optimism. You're asking 'Is this thought distorted or realistic?' and 'What interpretation fits the evidence?' — not 'How can I make this sound positive?'

questions.question: Choose a recent automatic thought that caused significant distress. Use the 7-step thought record: (1) Situation, (2) Emotion + intensity, (3) Automatic thought, (4) Evidence FOR, (5) Evidence AGAINST, (6) Balanced alternative, (7) Re-rate emotion. How much did your distress decrease?

title: The CBT Model: Evidence-Based Thought Change - Quiz
